name: BSD Zero Clause License
blurb: Public-domain-equivalent permissive license. Grants use, copy, modify, and distribute with no conditions at all, not even attribution. Derived from ISC with all obligations removed.

name: Apache License 2.0
blurb: Permissive license with an explicit patent grant and a defensive termination clause. Ubiquitous in enterprise open source.

name: BSD 2-Clause License
blurb: Simplified or 'FreeBSD' BSD license. Permits redistribution with or without modification as long as the copyright notice and disclaimer are retained. No patent grant; no endorsement clause.

name: BSD 3-Clause "New" or "Revised" License
blurb: Revised BSD. Same as BSD 2-Clause plus a 'no endorsement' clause: contributors' names may not be used to promote derivatives without permission. No patent grant.

name: BSD 4-Clause "Original" or "Old" License
blurb: Historical 'original' BSD license. Adds the notorious advertising clause (clause 3): every advertising material mentioning features or use of the software must carry an acknowledgement of the developer. Not OSI-approved; FSF classifies it as non-free and GPL-incompatible. Superseded by the 3-clause BSD, which dropped the advertising clause.

name: Boost Software License 1.0
blurb: Short permissive license used by the Boost C++ libraries. Like MIT but with a notable carve-out: copyright and license notices only need to accompany source-form redistributions, not machine-executable object code. GPL-compatible.

name: Community Data License Agreement - Permissive, Version 2.0
blurb: Linux Foundation's permissive data license. Widely used in ML training datasets. Allows use and modification with a minimal notice obligation; Results (e.g. trained models) are unrestricted.

name: CERN Open Hardware Licence Version 2 - Permissive
blurb: Permissive variant of the CERN Open Hardware Licence v2. Design files and products may be redistributed, modified, and Conveyed under different licence terms, with Notices retained and an explicit patent grant. No source-disclosure or share-alike obligation, unlike the -W and -S variants.

name: ISC License
blurb: Minimalist permissive license functionally equivalent to the simplified BSD and MIT licenses. Default license for OpenBSD and many npm packages.

name: MIT License
blurb: Shortest permissive license. Commercial and proprietary use permitted with attribution.

name: Microsoft Public License
blurb: Microsoft's short permissive license with an explicit patent grant, a patent-retaliation clause, a 'no trademark license' clause, and a notice-retention requirement. Free software per FSF but GPL-incompatible.

name: Python Software Foundation License 2.0
blurb: Permissive license under which CPython is distributed. Requires retaining the PSF copyright notice and documenting modifications with a brief summary of changes. No explicit patent grant. GPL-compatible per FSF.

name: Universal Permissive License 1.0
blurb: Oracle's preferred permissive license. MIT-style simplicity plus a broad explicit patent grant covering both the unmodified Software and any 'Larger Work' the licensor contributes it to. GPL-compatible.

name: zlib License
blurb: Very short permissive license. Permits any use including commercial, with three source-distribution constraints: do not misrepresent the origin, plainly mark altered versions, and keep the notice in source. No obligations on binary redistribution.

name: Artistic License 2.0
blurb: Perl's preferred license. Weak copyleft with menu-style compliance: distributors of Modified Versions choose among contributing upstream, renaming and not clobbering the Standard Version, or granting downstream source redistribution rights. Includes an explicit patent grant and retaliation clause.

name: Common Development and Distribution License 1.0
blurb: File-level weak copyleft derived from MPL 1.1. Each CDDL file stays under CDDL, but a Larger Work can combine CDDL and non-CDDL files under other terms. Deliberately GPL-incompatible; the reason ZFS cannot be upstreamed into Linux.

name: CERN Open Hardware Licence Version 2 - Weakly Reciprocal
blurb: Weakly reciprocal variant of the CERN Open Hardware Licence v2. Modified Covered Source must be released under CERN-OHL-W, but Available Components and External Material interfaced via a documented interface may remain under their own licences, and Products themselves may be licensed under terms of Your choice.

name: Eclipse Public License 1.0
blurb: Module-level weak copyleft with patent grant and retaliation. Predecessor to EPL-2.0; lacks the Secondary License mechanism, so it is GPL-incompatible by design.

name: Eclipse Public License 2.0
blurb: Module-level weak copyleft with patent grant and retaliation. Adds an opt-in Secondary License mechanism (Exhibit A) so the initial contributor can declare GPL-compatibility.

name: European Union Public License 1.2
blurb: EU-drafted copyleft license with two distinctive features: a Compatibility clause (§5 + Appendix) that lets EUPL code flow into projects under GPL-2.0/3.0, AGPL-3.0, MPL-2.0, EPL-1.0 and others; and a broad 'Communication' definition that triggers source disclosure on network deployment, similar in effect to AGPL §13.

name: GNU Lesser General Public License v2.1 only
blurb: Weak copyleft for libraries. Modifications to the library must be LGPL, but applications that merely link against the library may be licensed freely. No explicit patent grant.

name: GNU Lesser General Public License v3.0 only
blurb: Weak copyleft layered over GPLv3. Modifications to the library must be LGPL/GPL; a Combined Work that merely links the library may be licensed freely. Inherits GPLv3 patent grant.

name: Mozilla Public License 2.0
blurb: File-level weak copyleft with an explicit patent grant. Modified MPL files stay MPL, but a larger work combining MPL files with other code can ship under different terms.

name: Microsoft Reciprocal License
blurb: File-level weak copyleft from Microsoft. Any file that contains Ms-RL code must ship its source under Ms-RL, but files entirely your own may carry any terms. Explicit patent grant with retaliation; no trademark license. GPL-incompatible.

name: GNU Affero General Public License v3.0 only
blurb: Strong copyleft that closes the SaaS loophole: §13 requires network users of modified versions to be offered the Corresponding Source, making AGPL the GPL for hosted software.

name: CERN Open Hardware Licence Version 2 - Strongly Reciprocal
blurb: Strongly reciprocal variant of the CERN Open Hardware Licence v2. Including Covered Source in a larger work makes the whole design modified Covered Source: the entire Complete Source of any Product must be released under CERN-OHL-S, with only Available Components excluded.

name: GNU General Public License v2.0 only
blurb: The original strong copyleft. Derivative works of GPL-licensed software must be distributed under GPL. No explicit patent grant. Silent on network/SaaS use.

name: GNU General Public License v3.0 only
blurb: Strong copyleft with an explicit patent grant, a patent retaliation clause, and anti-tivoization rules. Silent on network use (AGPL closes that gap).

name: Creative Commons Attribution 2.0 Generic
blurb: CC Attribution 2.0 — Flickr's default license for years; lots of legacy content. Permits commercial use, modification, distribution with credit.

name: Creative Commons Attribution 2.5 Generic
blurb: Older CC Attribution 2.5. Transitional version, jurisdiction-ported in many countries. Permits commercial use, modification, distribution with credit.

name: Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 Unported
blurb: Predecessor of CC-BY-4.0. Permits commercial use, modification, and distribution if you credit the author, keep licence notices, and label any changes. Silent on patents and the licensor's own trademarks, which 4.0 later made explicit. Widely used for media and content across the 2007-2013 era.

name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
blurb: Attribution license for creative works. Permits use, modification, and distribution, including commercially, provided credit is given and modifications are indicated. No share-alike. Patent and trademark rights are not granted.

name: Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 2.0
blurb: Older CC-BY-NC 2.0. Restricts to noncommercial use only. Classified nonfree by FSF.

name: Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 International
blurb: Creative Commons attribution license that forbids commercial use. Permits modification and distribution for non-commercial purposes with credit. Not OSI-approved; classified nonfree by FSF.

name: Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivs 2.0
blurb: Most restrictive CC 2.0 variant. NC + ND. Nonfree per FSF.

name: Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 International
blurb: Most restrictive Creative Commons license. Permits only verbatim non-commercial sharing with attribution. Distribution of modifications is forbidden. Not OSI-approved; classified nonfree by FSF.

name: Creative Commons Attribution-NoDerivs 2.0
blurb: CC-BY-ND 2.0. Permits distribution of verbatim copies but forbids derivatives. Classified nonfree by FSF for works of practical use.

name: Creative Commons Attribution-NoDerivatives 4.0 International
blurb: Attribution license for creative works that forbids sharing derivatives. Permits commercial use and redistribution of verbatim copies with credit; adapted material may be produced privately but not distributed.

name: Open Data Commons Attribution License v1.0
blurb: Attribution-only sibling of ODbL. Permits use, modification, and sharing of databases provided the Producer is credited. No share-alike obligation.

name: SIL Open Font License 1.1
blurb: Copyleft-style license for fonts. Fonts may be used, modified, embedded, and sold bundled with software, but not sold on their own. Modified versions must use a different name from the original and must be released under the OFL.

name: Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0
blurb: Older CC-BY-NC-SA 2.0. Adds ShareAlike to NC.

name: Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International
blurb: Creative Commons copyleft license for non-commercial use. Adapted works must be shared under the same (or compatible) BY-NC-SA license. Not OSI-approved; classified nonfree by FSF.

name: Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 2.0 Generic
blurb: CC ShareAlike 2.0. Predecessor to 2.5/3.0/4.0. Adaptations must be CC-BY-SA.

name: Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 2.5 Generic
blurb: Older CC ShareAlike 2.5. Adds ShareAlike to BY-2.5. Legacy license attached to 2005-2007 era content.

name: Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 3.0 Unported
blurb: Copyleft for creative works. Adds ShareAlike to CC-BY-3.0: adaptations must be distributed under this License, a later version with the same License Elements, a CC jurisdiction license, or a Creative Commons Compatible License. Used by Wikipedia until 2023 and by Stack Overflow content from 2009 to 2018.

name: Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 4.0 International
blurb: Copyleft license for creative works. Permits use, modification, and commercial distribution provided credit is given, changes are indicated, and derivatives are released under CC-BY-SA or a Creative Commons-designated compatible license.

name: Community Data License Agreement - Sharing, Version 1.0
blurb: Data copyleft variant of CDLA. Data-focused analog of ShareAlike; Enhanced Data must be Published under CDLA-Sharing. Results of computational analysis are exempt.

name: GNU Free Documentation License v1.3 only
blurb: FSF's copyleft license for documentation. Modified versions must be released under GFDL with preserved History, copyright notices, and any Invariant Sections. Section 3 requires Transparent (editable) copies alongside Opaque distributions above 100 units.

name: Open Database License (ODbL) v1.0
blurb: Share-alike copyleft for databases. OpenStreetMap's license. Requires Adapted Databases to be under ODbL or a compatible license.

name: TAPR Open Hardware License 1.0
blurb: Share-alike hardware license from TAPR (2007). Distributors of Products must ship the Documentation (schematics, Gerber, CAD/CAM) or make it available for three years, and modifications must be licensed under the OHL. Uses a mutual covenant-not-to-sue on patents rather than an affirmative patent grant.

name: Creative Commons Zero v1.0 Universal
blurb: Public-domain dedication with a fallback permissive license. FSF-approved and GPL-compatible, but not OSI-approved because §4(a) expressly declines to grant patent rights.

name: Open Data Commons Public Domain Dedication and License v1.0
blurb: CC0-equivalent for databases. Waives all rights as public-domain dedication with fallback licence for jurisdictions that do not recognise dedications.

name: The Unlicense
blurb: Public-domain dedication with a permissive fallback license. Intended to place software outside copyright entirely; no attribution, no notice preservation.

name: Do What The F*ck You Want To Public License
blurb: Minimal public-domain-equivalent license, one operative sentence long. No attribution, no conditions, and notably no warranty disclaimer. FSF-approved as very lax permissive but discouraged in favor of CC0; not OSI-approved.

name: Business Source License 1.1
blurb: Source-available license with a built-in time delay: non-production use is permitted immediately, and on the Change Date (no later than four years after first release) the work converts to a specified Change License (typically GPLv2-or-later-compatible). Used by MariaDB, Sentry, CockroachDB, and others.

name: Elastic License 2.0
blurb: Source-available license used by Elastic, Kibana, and others. Grants broad rights to use, modify, and distribute the software but forbids offering it as a hosted or managed service and forbids circumventing commercial license keys. Includes an express patent grant with defensive termination.

name: PolyForm Noncommercial License 1.0.0
blurb: Source-available license from the PolyForm Project. Permits use, modification, distribution, and patent rights only for noncommercial purposes: personal, charitable, educational, public research, public safety, environmental, or government use. Commercial use requires a separate license.

name: Server Side Public License 1.0
blurb: MongoDB's AGPL-derived license. Fork of AGPL-3.0 with §13 rewritten: offering the software as a service requires releasing the ENTIRE service-stack source (management, UI, API, automation, monitoring, backup, storage, hosting). Rejected by OSI as non-open-source.
